title: Tandoori chicken
subtitle: Yoghurt-marinated chicken grilled until charred
excerpt: Chicken legs marinated overnight in spiced yoghurt with garlic and ginger, then grilled at high heat until charred and smoky with a crisp, fragrant crust. The grill does what a tandoor oven would — not the same, but close enough. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Tandoori chicken was created at Moti Mahal restaurant in Delhi in the 1940s by Kundan Lal Gujral, who adapted the Punjabi tandoor technique for a restaurant kitchen. Documented by Madhur Jaffrey in An Invitation to Indian Cooking (1973) and Camellia Panjabi in 50 Great Curries of India (1994). The domestic grill version produces a credible result — the key is maximum heat and a long marinade.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate overnight for the best result — 8 hours minimum.

content.text: The overnight marinade is not optional. The yoghurt tenderises the chicken and carries the spices deep into the flesh; a one-hour marinade produces a dish that tastes predominantly of yoghurt. Score the chicken deeply before marinating (the cuts should reach the bone) so the marinade penetrates the thickest parts.

At the grilling stage, use the highest heat your grill can produce. The marinade needs to char at the edges and the skin needs to colour to near-black in places. This is not burning — it is where the flavour comes from. The chicken will not dry out because the yoghurt marinade has formed a protective layer.

items.prepNote: bone-in, skin-on, scored deeply to the bone

content.text: Score the chicken thighs deeply with a sharp knife, making cuts 2 cm apart down to the bone. Combine {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt with {{garlic}} of grated garlic, {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{garam-masala}} of garam masala, {{turmeric}} of ground turmeric, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, the lemon juice, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Mix well. Rub the marinade thoroughly over and into the scored chicken, working it into the cuts. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours, or overnight.

Remove the chicken from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Heat the grill to its maximum setting. Place the chicken pieces on a foil-lined roasting tin. Grill for 15 minutes, then turn and grill for another 15–20 minutes until cooked through and charred at the edges. Check by inserting a skewer at the thickest point — the juices should run clear. Rest for 5 minutes before serving with lemon wedges, sliced raw onion, and coriander.

items.symptom: Chicken cooked through but no colour or char
items.cause: Grill not hot enough, or chicken placed too far from the element.
items.fix: Position the chicken as close to the grill element as possible — the rack should be on the highest shelf. Maximum heat is non-negotiable for tandoori-style cooking.

items.cause: Chicken not scored deeply enough, or marinade too thin.
items.fix: Score to the bone — the cuts hold the marinade. If the yoghurt was watery, strain it through a cloth for 30 minutes before using.

items.symptom: Chicken dry inside despite the marinade
items.cause: Overcooked, or breast meat used instead of thigh.
items.fix: Thigh meat is far more forgiving than breast under a grill. If using breast, reduce the grilling time and check temperature — 74°C at the centre is done.

content.text: Tandoori chicken was one of the first Indian dishes to cross over into mainstream British consciousness, served in the 1960s and 1970s in the wave of Bangladeshi and Punjabi restaurants that opened across British cities. The bright orange-red colour from the original recipe used artificial food colouring; smoked paprika achieves a similar effect without it. The dish is a Punjabi import: the clay tandoor oven is native to the Punjab, used for bread and meat. In its original context it is as simple as grilled chicken gets, the yoghurt marinade being the only preparation needed.
